title: Algar's Central Coast
content: Algar Central Coast#A regional map of Algar’s central coast, showing the lands, settlements, and geography familiar to anyone who has grown up in this part of the world.

title: Bridgeport
content:
Bridgeport#Bridgeport is a freehold city of approximately 8,000 souls situated at the mouth of the Godsfall River where it meets Pell’s Harbor, on the western coast of Algar. It is a city of commerce, ambition, and barely contained chaos — a place where fortunes are made and lost, where every faction has a foothold, and where the streets themselves seem to have opinions.
Governance#Bridgeport is governed by the Council of Trades, a body of merchant representatives whose authority derives from coin rather than crown. The current High Convenor is Seraphina Velcross, a sharp political mind who maintains an uneasy balance between the city’s many competing interests.
The Crown maintains a garrison at Fort Royal, commanded by Colonel Alastair Thorneveil. The garrison’s presence is a reminder that Bridgeport’s independence has limits — though most days, the Colonel and the Council maintain a careful mutual tolerance.
Districts & Landmarks#Saltstone Square#The beating heart of Bridgeport’s street life, Saltstone Square is a crowded market district where merchants, laborers, and trouble all congregate. The Strangled Goat tavern sits on its edge — a reliable place for a drink, a rumor, or an ill-advised conversation.
Riverbend Mills#The industrial district along Stonewash Creek, where the city’s grain is ground and its goods are processed. Not glamorous, but essential.
Northwood Stands#The dense woodland that borders Bridgeport to the north. Few go in without good reason, and not all who do come back.
Faith#Three temples serve Bridgeport’s spiritual needs — or at least its official ones:
Temple of Radiant Light — devoted to Aard Shrine of Iron Oath — devoted to Kelleth Umbral House — devoted to Noctis Atmosphere#Bridgeport smells like salt, fish, and ambition. Its streets are loud, its politics are complicated, and its people are pragmatic above all else. It is not a safe city, but it is an interesting one — and for those with the nerve to navigate it, full of opportunity.

title: Council of Trades
content:
Council of Trades#The Council of Trades is the governing body of Bridgeport — a assembly of merchant representatives whose authority derives from commerce rather than birthright. In Bridgeport, coin is power, and the Council is where that power is formally organized.
The Council manages the city’s laws, its markets, its harbor fees, and its relationships with the Crown and neighboring settlements. It is not a democracy in any simple sense — representation on the Council is tied to trade volume and guild membership — but it is at least predictable, which counts for a great deal in a city like Bridgeport.
Leadership#The Council is presided over by the High Convenor, currently Seraphina Velcross — a sharp, composed woman with a reputation for finding compromises that leave everyone slightly dissatisfied, which in Bridgeport politics counts as a success.
The Crown’s Role#Bridgeport’s status as a freehold means the Council governs independently in most matters. However, the Crown maintains a garrison at Fort Royal under Colonel Alastair Thorneveil, and certain matters — particularly those involving trade disputes with Crown territories or questions of military security — fall outside the Council’s sole authority.
The relationship between the Council and the garrison is professionally cordial and privately complicated. Both parties prefer it that way.
Dealing with the Council#For most residents of Bridgeport, the Council is a distant abstraction — something that sets the harbor fees and occasionally posts new ordinances. For merchants, guild members, and anyone with significant business interests in the city, however, Council relationships are everything.
Those seeking official sanction for unusual activities, letters of passage, or resolution of significant disputes will eventually find themselves needing Council attention. Getting that attention is rarely simple.

title: The Gods
content:
The Gods#The divine order of the Moonshroud Realms flows from a single source: Spherus, the creator, who imagined nine Eternals into being at the founding of the world. The Eternals are not gods in the conventional sense — they are fundamental forces given will and purpose, each responsible for a domain of reality rather than a nation of worshippers.
Beneath the Eternals exist the Ascendants — beings of mortal origin who were elevated to divinity through extraordinary deeds, divine selection, or profound sacrifice. Ascendants are more accessible to mortal understanding than the Eternals, and their stories are told in temples, around fires, and in the songs of traveling bards.
In Bridgeport, three temples serve the city’s spiritual life openly. Others are known but less visible.
The Eternals#Aard#Eternal of Truth, Knowledge, History, and Reason
Aard is among the most actively worshipped of the Eternals in Bridgeport and across Algar. The pursuit of truth, the preservation of history, and the application of reason to the problems of the world are Aard’s domain. Scholars, judges, scribes, and magistrates count themselves among the faithful.
Symbol: An all-seeing eye crowned by a flame of enlightenment, set above a scroll of recorded memory.
Temple in Bridgeport: The Temple of Radiant Light on Queensway Avenue — the largest and most respected temple in the city, functioning as both a place of worship and one of the finest libraries in the Freehold.
Kelleth#Eternal of War, Justice, and Duty
Kelleth embodies the harder virtues — the discipline of the soldier, the binding weight of an oath, and the understanding that justice without duty is merely preference. Fighters, guards, militia officers, and adventurers often seek Kelleth’s blessing before dangerous work. The shrine is also a place for binding contracts and formal accords.
Symbol: A warhammer set before a scroll, encircled by an unbroken ring.
Temple in Bridgeport: The Shrine of the Iron Oath — its priests serve as neutral stewards during contract signings and oath ceremonies. The spoken word, in Kelleth’s house, is treated as steel.
Absot#Eternal of Cause and Effect, Cosmic Order, and the Unbroken Chain
Absot governs inevitability — the unbreakable relationship between action and consequence. Absot does not weigh good and evil; it ensures only that nothing occurs without reason and no choice is ever without outcome. Devotion to Absot is less about seeking comfort and more about seeking clarity — understanding where one’s actions lead, and accepting what cannot be changed.
Symbol: Geometric patterns suggesting progression and consequence — linear motifs of the unbroken chain.
Temple in Wexford: The Marble Canon, presided over by Hierophant Halwen Marr, is the primary temple of Absot in the region.
The Ascendants#Noctis#Ascendant of Shadowed Thresholds, Hidden Truths, and the Quiet Places Between Waking and Dream
Noctis began as a mortal druid of the Circle of the Seven Moons before being elevated by the Eternal Sik. Their path ran through sacrifice, shadow, and centuries of unseen service before ascension as sovereign of the Umbral — the mirror realm of shadow, memory, and endings. Noctis is neither malevolent nor comforting in the conventional sense; they preside over transitions, thresholds, and the truths that only become visible in the dark.
Those who visit the Umbral House come seeking guidance in matters other temples cannot address — protection from unseen dangers, clarity in deception, safe passage through darkness, or relief from troubling dreams.
Symbol: The Threefold Eclipse — three aligned discs of silver, grey, and black, representing the journey from light into shadow.
Temple in Bridgeport: The Umbral House, on the northern outskirts of the city. Its narrow windows, dim interior, and reputation for strange occurrences keep many away — which suits its faithful well enough.
Soryn#Ascendant of Sacred Guardianship, Restraint, and Mercy Earned Through Strength
Soryn was once Hrysa Bear-Bound, a shieldmaiden of the Sons of Rissen barbarian confederation of the northern plains. She earned the notice of Kelleth through not just her victories, but her restraint — she was merciless in defense of her own, yet would not abide slaughter for its own sake. After ascension, she is credited by the Ursinor with shaping their people, granting them mortality, kinship, and the right to choose their own paths rather than exist as endless instruments of war. Soryn is revered particularly among the Ursinor clans, whose devotion is quiet, habitual, and deeply personal.
A Note on the Divine#The distinction between Eternals and Ascendants matters in practice. Eternals are forces — vast, largely impersonal, and concerned with domains rather than individuals. Ascendants were once mortal, and their stories are human-scaled. They made choices, bore costs, and were changed by what they experienced. That history makes them easier to pray to.
Neither category guarantees a response. Faith in the Moonshroud Realms is a practice, not a transaction.
This page will expand as the party encounters more of the world’s divine landscape.

title: The World
content:
The World#The Moonshroud Realms is a world of ancient power and creeping decay — where the bones of civilization rest on foundations older than memory, and where something beneath the surface has begun, slowly and inexorably, to come apart.
The Shape of the World#The known world centers on Elderland, a vast continent of diverse peoples, competing kingdoms, and deep wilderness. Within Elderland lies Algar, a country of merchant cities, old forests, and Crown authority that grows thinner the further one travels from the capital.
On Algar’s western coast, where the Godsfall River meets Pell’s Harbor, sits Bridgeport — a freehold city of some 8,000 souls, and the place where this story begins.
The Peoples of the Realms#The Moonshroud Realms is home to many peoples. Among those the party has encountered:
The Aelfaen — a people with deep ties to the old forests, found in great numbers in the woodland settlements south of Bridgeport The Ursinor — bear-folk of the deep woodlands, organized by clan and governed by ancient tradition Humans, who are everywhere, in every role, at every level of society The Feeling of the World#This is not a world of clear heroes and obvious villains. Power is held by those who can keep it, justice is local and imperfect, and the old stories are not always wrong. Magic is real but not commonplace — it draws attention, carries cost, and is rarely as simple as it appears.
Something is wrong with the world at a fundamental level. The party may not know this yet. But they will.
Known Locations#Bridgeport — Freehold city at the mouth of the Godsfall River This page will grow as the party’s knowledge of the world expands.

title: Adventurer's Guild
content:
Adventurer’s Guild#The Adventurer’s Guild is one of the few organizations that operates openly across most of the known world, maintaining halls in cities and towns large enough to support one. Bridgeport’s chapter is well-established, reasonably well-funded, and busy enough that its staff have developed the particular brand of weary pragmatism common to people who spend their days sending others into danger.
What the Guild Does#The Guild serves as a clearinghouse for work — connecting those with problems requiring unconventional solutions to those willing to provide them, for appropriate compensation. It posts contracts, verifies completion, handles payment disputes, and maintains records of members in good standing.
For adventurers, Guild membership provides:
Access to posted contracts and bounties A degree of legal protection — Guild members operating on sanctioned contracts are afforded certain considerations under Bridgeport law Access to the Guild hall’s common room, notice boards, and basic facilities A reputation framework — Guild standing can open doors that would otherwise stay shut Membership#Joining the Guild requires a modest fee and the endorsement of an existing member or a Guild representative. New members are registered, issued a Guild mark, and assigned a probationary period during which their conduct is noted.
The Guild does not ask many questions about a member’s past. It asks a great many questions about their present conduct.
The Guild’s Limits#The Guild is not a mercenary army, a criminal organization, or a social club — though it has been mistaken for all three. It does not guarantee the safety of its members, the quality of its contract-givers, or the accuracy of information posted on its boards.
What it guarantees is payment for completed work, and consequences for those who fail to deliver on their end of a contract — whether contractor or client.
Caveat emptor, as they say in the old tongue. But at least someone is keeping score.

title: Factions
content: Factions#The organizations, guilds, gangs, and powers that shape life in the Moonshroud Realms.

title: The Moons
content:
The Seven Moons of Orb#The world of Orb turns beneath seven moons. Every person who has ever looked up at a clear night sky in the Moonshroud Realms has seen them — some bright, some faint, some moving faster than others across the dark. Most people know them as a fact of life, the way they know the seasons or the tides. Scholars and druids know them as something more.
What Is Known#The seven moons are not merely lights. Each has its own influence, its own rhythm, and its own consequence for the world below. Sailors read them for navigation. Farmers watch them for planting cycles. Druids study the patterns of their conjunctions and oppositions as carefully as any text. Astrologers argue endlessly about what each alignment means.
The Circle of the Seven Moons was a druidic order devoted entirely to mapping the rhythms of Orb’s seven satellites and interpreting the tides of magic they cast across the world. Their knowledge was deep and carefully accumulated over generations. Much of it has not survived in accessible form.
What is commonly understood is this: when multiple moons align, something in the world responds. Magic behaves differently. Certain creatures grow restless. The boundaries between Orb and the mirror planes — the Umbral and Haelo — grow thinner or thicker depending on which moons are prominent. Whether this is a property of the moons themselves or of what lies behind them is a question scholars have not settled.
What Remains Unknown#The full names, individual properties, and specific influences of each of the seven moons are knowledge that has not yet fully reached the party. This page will expand as the campaign progresses and more is learned.
There are people in the world who know more. Finding them is another matter.
This entry will be updated as the party’s understanding of the moons and their significance grows.

title: West Bridgeport
content: West Bridgeport City Map#A map of West Bridgeport, the freehold city at the mouth of the Godsfall River.

title: Wexford
content:
Wexford#Wexford is a walled woodland town of approximately 1,000 souls, situated some fifty miles south of Bridgeport in the depths of the Wexwood Forest. It sits at the confluence of the Wex River and Spring Creek, and is predominantly home to the Aelfaen — though travellers, traders, and those with business in the forest pass through regularly enough that outsiders are not unknown.
It is a quieter place than Bridgeport, but quieter does not mean simple.
Governance#Wexford operates under a layered authority that keeps several interests in careful balance:
Seneschal Maereth Ilthandor represents the Crown and governs from Sands House Mayor Thomas Greenweald handles civic matters and speaks for the townspeople A Council of Six advises on local affairs The Crown garrison is quartered at the Crown Barracks, commanded by Captain Aurix Valemor — a small but professional force whose presence reminds visitors that Wexford, like Bridgeport, exists within the reach of Algar’s authority.
Key Locations#Inns & Taverns#The Limping Fiddler — the town’s primary inn, run by Elowen Thistledown The Gilded Alder — a more upscale establishment for those with coin to spare Institutions#The Guild Hall — center of Wexford’s commercial life Camber House — local seat of the R.A. Society The Copper Hospice — a place of healing run by Matron Ilyra Fencrest, though there is more to it than meets the eye Feyward Spire — an ancient structure whose purpose is not entirely clear Waywarden Beacon — a landmark for travellers navigating the Wexwood Temples#Marble Canon — temple of Absot, overseen by Hierophant Halwen Marr Lantern-Keeper Temple — devoted to Mollo The Wexwood#The forest surrounding Wexford is old — older than the town, older than the roads, possibly older than memory. The Aelfaen who live here have a relationship with it that outsiders rarely fully understand. It is not hostile, exactly. But it is watchful.
Travellers are advised to stay on marked paths.
Notes#Wexford has not yet been visited by the party. This entry will expand as they learn more.

title: People
content: People#Notable individuals the party has encountered — allies, enemies, and everyone in between.

title: The Kangs
content:
The Kangs#Bridgeport has a lot of things adults don’t pay much attention to. The Kangs are one of them.
They are three groups of adolescent girls — identified by color, red, blue, or yellow — who move through the city’s streets, markets, harbor front, and civic quarter with the easy confidence of people who belong everywhere and nowhere in particular. Adults barely register them. That is, by all appearances, exactly the point.
What People Notice#Each Kang wears a single marker in her group’s color — a ribbon, a cord, a strip of cloth — in a consistent location. Wrist, ankle, collar. The item and its placement mean something to those who know how to read them. To everyone else, it’s decoration.
There are also those in white markers. New members, perhaps. Or something else. It’s hard to say.
What People Know#The three groups have different territories and different styles. The ones near the harbor are bold. The ones near the guild streets are quiet and watchful. The ones in the market lanes seem to know everyone and everything.
They are not violent, exactly. They are not to be trifled with, exactly. Most people in Bridgeport have settled on a comfortable working relationship of mutual non-interference, which suits everyone well enough.
They talk to each other in ways that outsiders don’t fully follow. They notice things. They remember things.
Whether any of that is useful depends entirely on whether they decide you’re worth talking to.
The party’s understanding of the Kangs will grow over time. This entry will be updated as they learn more.

title: The World Beyond
content:
The World Beyond Bridgeport#Bridgeport sits at the mouth of the Godsfall River on the western coast of Algar, one nation among many on the continent of Elderland. It is a busy, prosperous city — and it is also a very small part of a very large world.
The Continent of Elderland#Elderland is the known world for most of its inhabitants. It is vast enough that few people ever see more than a fraction of it in a lifetime, and large enough that its eastern reaches are more legend than geography to those living on the western coast.
Algar occupies a significant portion of Elderland’s western reaches. It is a parliamentary monarchy, governed by a Crown whose authority extends across hereditary Duchies and Baronies — except in places like Bridgeport, whose Freehold status grants it unusual autonomy. The capital is Knightsbridge, far to the south.
Beyond Algar lie other nations, other peoples, and other histories. The continent has been shaped by centuries of conflict, migration, and the rise and fall of civilizations whose names survive only in the oldest archives.
Beyond Elderland#The world does not end at Elderland’s shores. Other continents lie across the seas, each with its own character:
Issitania — Scorched and sun-bound. A land of heat, endurance, and ancient power baked into the stone.
Lakh-Ur — Ancient and unyielding. Whatever shaped Lakh-Ur shaped it a very long time ago, and it has not forgotten.
Saturna — Distant and half-mythical. Few who speak of Saturna have been there. Whether the stories are accurate is difficult to verify.
Ships leave Bridgeport’s harbor for distant ports. Some of those voyages eventually reach these shores. Most sailors have opinions about what they find there.
The Mirror Planes#Beyond the material world lie two mirror realms — dimensions that reflect and refract Orb in different ways:
The Umbral — A realm of shadow, memory, and endings. The Umbral exists alongside the material world as its darker reflection, a place where boundaries blur and old truths linger. It is presided over by Noctis. Those who venture there find that the Umbral remembers things the material world has forgotten.
Haelo — A realm of growth, excess, and untamed possibility. Haelo is the fey mirror — wilder, stranger, and less bound by the rules that govern mortal existence. The goblins of the Gutterdeep Warrens are said to have slipped into the world through a fey crossing at Silmar, driven out of Haelo by forces they refuse to name clearly.
The boundaries between Orb and these mirror planes are not always stable. Thin places exist where the walls grow weak — and what lies on the other side does not always wait to be invited through.
A Note on Scale#Most of this is background knowledge — the kind of thing a well-traveled merchant or a well-read scholar might know. The party’s story begins in Bridgeport. The wider world exists, it presses in at the edges, and it will make itself felt in time.
For now, the Godsfall River is running, the harbor is busy, and there is work to be done.
